title: Install dependencies and set up project structure
description: Install all required Python packages and create the project directory structure for experiments, results, and figures.

steps.step1: Create a virtual environment or use the existing Python environment. Install packages: numpy, scipy, pandas, matplotlib, seaborn, scikit-learn, networkx, causal-learn (pip install causallearn), statsmodels, tqdm, joblib.
steps.step2: Create directory structure: src/ (source code), results/ (raw results), figures/ (paper figures), data/ (cached datasets).
steps.step3: Create src/e_values.py — implements e-value computation: (a) calibrator-based e-values from p-values using universal calibrator e = 1/(p * e * log(1/p)) for p < 1/e, else 0; (b) split-likelihood-ratio e-values using data splitting (fit model on first half, evaluate likelihood ratio on second half).
steps.step4: Create src/e_ci_tests.py — implements e-value CI tests: (a) E-value Fisher-z test for linear Gaussian data (compute partial correlation, get p-value via Fisher-z, convert to e-value via calibrator, OR compute split-LR e-value); (b) E-value kernel CI test for nonlinear data (split data, fit kernel ridge regression on first half, compute likelihood ratio on second half).
steps.step5: Create src/e_pc.py — implements the E-PC algorithm: modified PC-stable that uses e-value CI tests, accumulates evidence across K folds, computes edge-specific e-values, and applies e-BH procedure for FDR-controlled edge selection.
steps.step6: Create src/pc_p.py — implements the PC-p baseline (Strobl et al., 2019): PC algorithm that computes edge-specific p-values (maximum p-value across CI tests for each edge) and applies BY FDR correction.
steps.step7: Create src/data_generation.py — implements synthetic data generation: linear Gaussian, linear non-Gaussian (exponential noise), and nonlinear additive noise models on random DAGs (Erdos-Renyi and scale-free).
steps.step8: Create src/metrics.py — implements evaluation metrics: SHD, FDR (false discovery rate = false edges / total reported edges), TPR (true positive rate = true edges / total true edges), F1, precision, orientation accuracy.
steps.step9: Create src/utils.py — utility functions for DAG generation (ER and SF graphs), CPDAG conversion, result I/O, and seed management.
steps.step10: Verify installation by running a small test: generate a 5-node ER graph, sample 200 observations, run standard PC, compute metrics.

title: Generate synthetic datasets and load real-world benchmarks
description: Pre-generate all synthetic datasets with fixed seeds for reproducibility, and load standard benchmark networks (Sachs, ALARM, Asia, Insurance) for real-data experiments.

steps.step1: Define experimental grid for synthetic data: graph types = {ER, SF}; num_nodes p = {10, 20, 50}; average_degree = {2, 4}; sample_sizes n = {200, 500, 1000, 2000}; data_types = {linear_gaussian, nonlinear_additive}; seeds = {42, 123, 456}. Total: 2 × 3 × 2 × 4 × 2 × 3 = 288 synthetic datasets.
steps.step2: For each configuration, generate: (a) a random DAG using the specified graph type and degree; (b) edge weights sampled uniformly from [-1, -0.5] ∪ [0.5, 1]; (c) data sampled from the structural equation model. For linear_gaussian: X_i = sum(w_ji * X_j) + N(0, 1). For nonlinear_additive: X_i = sum(tanh(w_ji * X_j)) + N(0, 0.5).
steps.step3: Save each dataset as a dictionary: {'data': np.array, 'true_dag': np.array, 'true_cpdag': np.array, 'config': dict}. Save to data/synthetic/ with naming convention: {graph_type}_{p}_{degree}_{n}_{data_type}_seed{seed}.npz.
steps.step4: Load real-world benchmark networks from causal-learn's built-in datasets or manually specify adjacency matrices: (a) Asia (8 nodes, 8 edges) — generate 5000 samples from known BN parameters; (b) Sachs (11 nodes, 17 edges) — load the observational dataset (853 samples); (c) ALARM (37 nodes, 46 edges) — generate 5000 samples from known BN parameters; (d) Insurance (27 nodes, 52 edges) — generate 5000 samples.
steps.step5: For real-world networks where ground truth is available as a BN, sample multiple datasets with different seeds (42, 123, 456) for repeated evaluation. Save to data/real/.
steps.step6: Compute and log summary statistics for all datasets: number of nodes, edges, samples, sparsity, max degree. Save to data/dataset_summary.csv.

title: Run baseline methods on all datasets
description: Run standard PC, PC-stable, PC-p (with BY correction), and GES on all synthetic and real datasets. These serve as baselines for comparison with E-PC.

steps.step1: Implement a unified experiment runner: run_experiment(method, data, true_dag, config) -> dict of metrics. Each method returns an estimated CPDAG and edge-level scores (p-values for PC-p, BIC scores for GES).
steps.step2: Run PC-stable (from causal-learn) with alpha values {0.01, 0.05, 0.1} on all synthetic datasets. Use Fisher-z test for linear_gaussian data. Record: estimated CPDAG, runtime, all metrics (SHD, FDR, TPR, F1, precision, orientation accuracy).
steps.step3: Run PC-p baseline on all synthetic datasets: (a) Run PC-stable to get edge-specific p-values (max p-value across all CI tests for each edge); (b) Apply BY correction at FDR levels q = {0.05, 0.1, 0.2}; (c) Remove edges that are not significant after BY correction. Record same metrics.
steps.step4: Run GES (from causal-learn) on all synthetic datasets with default BIC scoring. Record same metrics.
steps.step5: Run all baselines on real-world datasets (Asia, Sachs, ALARM, Insurance) with the same configurations.
steps.step6: Save all results to results/baselines/ as JSON files: one file per dataset with all method results. Format: {dataset_name: {method: {metrics: {...}, runtime: float, config: {...}}}}.
steps.step7: Estimated runtime: ~2 minutes per dataset × 288 synthetic × 4 methods / 2 cores ≈ 19 hours. SCOPE REDUCTION: Run linear_gaussian only for full grid; nonlinear_additive only for p={10,20}, n={500,1000}, degree=2. Reduces to ~180 effective dataset-method pairs × ~1 min = ~3 hours for baselines.

title: Run E-PC on all datasets
description: Run the proposed E-PC algorithm with e-value CI tests and e-BH FDR control on all synthetic and real datasets. Compare against baselines at matched FDR levels.

steps.step1: Run E-PC with calibrator-based e-values (default configuration): K=5 folds, FDR targets q={0.05, 0.1, 0.2}, using Fisher-z p-values converted via universal calibrator. Run on all synthetic datasets in the experimental grid.
steps.step2: Run E-PC with split-likelihood-ratio e-values: K=5 folds, FDR targets q={0.05, 0.1, 0.2}. For linear Gaussian data, use the split-LR approach (fit linear model on first half, compute LR on second half). Run on all linear_gaussian synthetic datasets.
steps.step3: Run E-PC on real-world datasets (Asia, Sachs, ALARM, Insurance) with both calibrator and split-LR e-values, K=5, q={0.05, 0.1, 0.2}.
steps.step4: For each run, record: estimated CPDAG, edge-specific e-values, fold-level e-values (for anytime analysis), runtime, all metrics (SHD, FDR, TPR, F1, precision, orientation accuracy).
steps.step5: Save results to results/epc/ with the same format as baselines.
steps.step6: Estimated runtime: E-PC has ~2-3x overhead vs PC due to data splitting and fold processing. ~3 min per dataset × ~180 datasets / 2 cores ≈ 4.5 hours. With scope reduction, target ~2.5 hours.

title: FDR calibration analysis
description: The core experiment: compare actual FDR vs nominal FDR across methods to test whether E-PC achieves well-calibrated FDR control while PC-p is overly conservative.

steps.step1: For each method and FDR target q, compute the empirical FDR averaged across all seeds for each experimental configuration. Empirical FDR = (number of false edges in estimated skeleton) / (total edges in estimated skeleton).
steps.step2: Create a calibration table: for each (method, q, graph_type, p, degree), compute mean and std of empirical FDR across seeds and sample sizes. A well-calibrated method should have empirical FDR ≈ q.
steps.step3: Compute calibration error: |empirical_FDR - q| for each method. Average across configurations to get overall calibration quality.
steps.step4: For standard PC (no FDR control), compute the empirical FDR at each alpha level to show that FDR is uncontrolled and grows with graph size.
steps.step5: Statistical test: for each configuration, test whether E-PC's empirical FDR is within 10% of the nominal level (i.e., empirical FDR ≤ 1.1 * q). Report the fraction of configurations satisfying this criterion (target: ≥ 80%).

title: Power analysis at matched FDR levels
description: Compare the number of true edges recovered (power/TPR) by E-PC vs PC-p at the same nominal FDR level. This tests the core claim that e-values provide higher power by avoiding conservative BY corrections.

steps.step1: For each nominal FDR level q = {0.05, 0.1, 0.2}, extract TPR for E-PC and PC-p across all datasets.
steps.step2: Compute paired differences in TPR (E-PC minus PC-p) for each dataset/seed combination.
steps.step3: Run paired t-test (or Wilcoxon signed-rank test) on TPR differences across all configurations. Report p-value and effect size (Cohen's d).
steps.step4: Break down power comparison by graph size (p), sample size (n), and graph type to identify where E-PC's advantage is largest.
steps.step5: Compute the relative improvement: (TPR_EPC - TPR_PCp) / TPR_PCp × 100% for each configuration.

title: Ablation study: e-value construction method
description: Compare calibrator-based e-values vs split-likelihood-ratio e-values to understand which construction yields better power while maintaining FDR control.

steps.step1: Select a subset of synthetic datasets: p={10, 20, 50}, degree=2, n={500, 1000}, linear_gaussian, all 3 seeds = 18 datasets.
steps.step2: Run E-PC with: (a) universal calibrator e-values; (b) split-likelihood-ratio e-values. Both with K=5 folds, q=0.1.
steps.step3: Compare metrics: FDR, TPR, F1, SHD for both e-value construction methods.
steps.step4: Analyze: calibrator approach is simpler but may lose power in conversion; split-LR is more direct but requires model fitting.

title: Ablation study: number of folds K
description: Study how the number of data folds K affects E-PC's power and FDR control. More folds allow more evidence accumulation but reduce per-fold sample size.

steps.step1: Select subset: p={10, 20}, degree=2, n={1000, 2000}, linear_gaussian, all 3 seeds = 12 datasets.
steps.step2: Run E-PC with K = {2, 3, 5, 8, 10} folds, q=0.1, using calibrator-based e-values.
steps.step3: For each K, record FDR, TPR, F1, and runtime.
steps.step4: Plot TPR and FDR as a function of K to identify the sweet spot.

title: Ablation study: anytime validity
description: Verify the anytime-valid property: demonstrate that stopping E-PC after processing K' < K folds still yields valid FDR control.

steps.step1: Select subset: p={10, 20, 50}, degree=2, n=1000, linear_gaussian, all 3 seeds = 9 datasets.
steps.step2: Run E-PC with K=10 folds, q=0.1. After each fold k=1,...,10, record the intermediate e-values and apply e-BH to get the graph at that stopping point.
steps.step3: For each stopping point k, compute empirical FDR and TPR.
steps.step4: Verify: FDR ≤ q at every stopping point (the anytime-valid property). TPR should increase monotonically with more folds.

title: Scalability analysis
description: Measure runtime of all methods as graph size increases to characterize the computational overhead of E-PC.

steps.step1: Generate additional datasets for scalability: p = {10, 20, 50, 100}, degree=2, n=1000, linear_gaussian, seed=42.
steps.step2: Run each method (PC-stable, PC-p, E-PC-calibrator, E-PC-split-LR, GES) and record wall-clock runtime.
steps.step3: Plot runtime vs number of nodes for each method.
steps.step4: Report the overhead factor of E-PC vs standard PC: runtime_EPC / runtime_PC.

title: Aggregate results and test success criteria
description: Compile all results and formally test each success criterion from the proposal.

steps.step1: Aggregate all metrics into a single DataFrame: columns = [method, graph_type, p, degree, n, data_type, seed, q, FDR, TPR, F1, SHD, precision, orientation_accuracy, runtime].
steps.step2: Test Success Criterion 1: 'E-PC controls FDR within 10% of nominal across ≥80% of configurations.' Count configurations where empirical_FDR ≤ 1.1 * q. Report fraction.
steps.step3: Test Success Criterion 2: 'E-PC recovers significantly more true edges than PC-p at matched FDR levels.' Run paired statistical test on TPR at each q level. Report p-value.
steps.step4: Test Success Criterion 3: 'Anytime validity holds at every stopping point.' Report max FDR violation across all stopping points and configurations.
steps.step5: Test Success Criterion 4: 'E-PC achieves competitive or superior SHD and F1 vs PC at best-tuned alpha.' Compare E-PC (q=0.1) vs PC-stable at best alpha. Report differences.
steps.step6: Test Success Criterion 5: 'Results hold across both linear Gaussian and nonlinear data.' Repeat criteria 1-2 separately for each data type.
steps.step7: Save comprehensive results table to results/all_results.csv and success criteria evaluation to results/success_criteria.json.

title: Generate paper figures and tables
description: Create publication-quality figures for the research paper covering FDR calibration, power comparison, ablations, and real-data results.

steps.step1: Figure 1 — FDR Calibration Plot: For each method, plot empirical FDR (y-axis) vs nominal FDR target q (x-axis) with error bars across seeds. Include a diagonal y=x line for reference. Separate panels for p={10, 20, 50}. Save as figures/fig1_fdr_calibration.pdf.
steps.step2: Figure 2 — Power Comparison: Bar chart or grouped plot showing TPR for each method at matched FDR levels (q=0.1). Group by graph size p. Include error bars across seeds. Save as figures/fig2_power_comparison.pdf.
steps.step3: Figure 3 — FDR-Power Tradeoff Curve: Plot TPR vs empirical FDR for each method by varying their respective thresholds (alpha for PC, q for PC-p and E-PC). This is analogous to an ROC curve. Save as figures/fig3_fdr_power_tradeoff.pdf.
steps.step4: Figure 4 — Anytime Validity: Plot empirical FDR and TPR as a function of number of folds processed (K'). Show that FDR stays below q at all stopping points while TPR increases. Save as figures/fig4_anytime_validity.pdf.
steps.step5: Figure 5 — Ablation: Number of Folds: Plot F1 score vs K for different graph sizes. Save as figures/fig5_ablation_folds.pdf.
steps.step6: Figure 6 — Scalability: Log-log plot of runtime vs number of nodes for each method. Save as figures/fig6_scalability.pdf.
steps.step7: Table 1 — Main Results Table: LaTeX table with SHD, FDR, TPR, F1 for all methods across key configurations (p={10,20,50}, n=1000, degree=2, linear_gaussian). Bold best results. Save as figures/table1_main_results.tex.
steps.step8: Table 2 — Real Data Results: LaTeX table with metrics for all methods on Asia, Sachs, ALARM, Insurance networks. Save as figures/table2_real_data.tex.
steps.step9: Table 3 — Ablation Summary: LaTeX table comparing e-value construction methods (calibrator vs split-LR). Save as figures/table3_ablation_evalue.tex.
steps.step10: All figures use matplotlib with seaborn styling, font size 12, tight layout, and colorblind-friendly palettes. Save both PDF and PNG versions.
